Name: info_theoretic_model_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Model Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of model compression can be characterized using a rate-distortion framework, where the distortion measure is tailored to the specific task and model architecture. By optimizing the trade-off between compression rate and distortion, we can achieve better compression ratios while preserving model performance.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored mixed-precision quantization and adaptive compression techniques for deep learning models. However, these approaches often rely on heuristic methods and lack a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Our proposal differs from existing work by providing a principled rate-distortion framework for model compression, which can be used to derive optimal compression strategies and evaluate their performance.
Abstract: Model compression is a crucial technique for reducing the computational and memory requirements of deep learning models. However, existing compression methods often rely on heuristic approaches and lack a rigorous theoretical foundation. In this proposal, we introduce an information-theoretic framework for model compression, which characterizes the fundamental limits of compression using a rate-distortion approach. By optimizing the trade-off between compression rate and distortion, we can achieve better compression ratios while preserving model performance. Our framework provides a principled way to derive optimal compression strategies and evaluate their performance, and can be applied to a wide range of deep learning models and tasks.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on several benchmark datasets and models to evaluate the performance of our rate-distortion framework for model compression. Specifically, we will: (1) derive optimal compression strategies for different models and tasks using our framework, (2) compare the performance of our approach with existing heuristic methods, and (3) evaluate the robustness of our approach to different distortion measures and compression ratios. We will use metrics such as compression ratio, model accuracy, and computational cost to evaluate the performance of our approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that our rate-distortion framework may not capture all the complexities of model compression, and may not always lead to optimal compression strategies. Another limitation is that our approach may require significant computational resources and expertise in information theory and deep learning. Additionally, our framework may not be directly applicable to all types of models and tasks, and may require modifications and extensions to accommodate different scenarios.
